jerome-masi.heroHook: Jérôme Masi paints Annecy calm—flat color, quiet childhoods, global commissions from a mountain town.
jerome-masi.storyFullText:
Jérôme Masi is an art director and illustrator in Annecy, represented by Creasenso, with a path that ran through games, Lyon studios, and motion before illustration took the wheel.

Commercial work stays digital for airlines, watches, and telecoms; personal paintings return to acrylic and easel silence.

He calls his taste sober and minimal—images that leave room for the viewer’s own memory to pour in.

moritz-adam-schmitt.heroHook: Moritz Adam Schmitt vectorized Cologne—flat color, street appetite, Adobe-era breakout.
moritz-adam-schmitt.storyFullText:
Moritz Adam Schmitt is a Cologne illustrator who earned attention the hard way: a year of daily icons, a MAX stage, then freelance life while the internet watched.

Research stacks into iPad roughs, then tight vector finishes—pop palettes, icon clarity, attitude borrowed from street posters.

The result is illustration that behaves like product design: bold, legible, impossible to mistake for anyone else.

loreta-isac.heroHook: Loreta Isac animates Bucharest dreams—illustration, motion, and the phrase ‘once upon a dreamer.’
loreta-isac.storyFullText:
Loreta Isac is a Bucharest illustrator and animator who still begins her story as a dreamer rooted in Iași before the capital.

Apple-sized clients sit beside journal pieces—frozen moments from travel, love, and weather you cannot name.

Her worlds are figurative and tender; motion is simply another way to let the drawing breathe.

dawal.heroHook: Dawal paints pocket dreams on wounded walls—Paris surrealism you almost step on.
dawal.storyFullText:
Dawal is a painter who lets the city’s cracks choose the composition. Surreal mini-scenes settle into broken plaster like they were always meant to live there.

Light, texture, and the odd geometry of urban decay steer each image; the story is rarely louder than the surface.

Childhood motifs drift through recent solos—memory and city skin pressed together.

antonia-lev.heroHook: Antonia Lev moves between Paris streets and the drawing table—illustration, murals, and motion-minded experiments.
antonia-lev.storyFullText:
Antonia Lev is an illustrator who never fully left the wall. Her practice folds street painting, editorial image-making, and restless digital tests into one lane.

The look is graphic and narrative: figures and scenes that hold up at mural scale and still intrigue on a page.

She has painted through lockdowns and festival summers alike—always chasing the same question: how much story fits on the surface in front of her.

Fine-art training slowed him down in the right ways: figure, landscape, and mural scale now share one painterly conscience.

New pieces keep appearing from hometown lots to distant cities—each wall another sentence in the same novel.

carsten-gueth.storyFullText:
Carsten Gueth publishes as Die Doing, a shorthand for image-making that still carries architecture school’s discipline.

Years inside agencies on cultural campaigns taught him speed and storytelling; independence let him chase stranger briefs—books, brands, walls, festivals.

The work is bold, slightly absurd, and always built to read from across the room.

or-bar-el.storyFullText:
Or Bar-El is an Israeli illustrator and animator whose SketchBoom project turns performance into image—camera on the hand, audience on the line.

Bezalel animation training and a decorated student film launched a path through tech and back toward illustration and walls.

The through-line is immediacy: drawing as event, not artifact.

Ori Toor is a Tel Aviv illustrator and animator who lets improvisation lead. Dense drawings grow like plants—one line suggests the next.

Commercial commissions share the same fertile mess; color refuses to behave.

Fans describe the work as music you can see: repetitive, escalating, strangely calm at the center.

Linda Baritski spent years art-directing inside major studios before SEASONOFVICTORY became her public name.

Murals, packaging, editorial, and brand systems now share one bold, pop-literate hand—Google to LEGO to the wall around the corner.

The through-line is confidence: color that commits, characters that swagger, drawing that knows its audience.

Erez Sameach works as Erezoo from Haifa, where the studio feels like a family workshop stretched across concrete.

Born in Carmiel, trained at Vizcoa, he helped build the SIRA collective alongside relatives who paint the same streets.

His images are loud, graphic, and rooted in coastal city life—posters, walls, and brand worlds that still smell like sea air.

Agus Rucula is an urban painter and teacher who uses scale, gesture, and the figure to open conversations in public space.

Her murals borrow from dance and photography as much as from spray and brush—communities often step into the work as participants, not just viewers.

The through-line is empathy at mural size: bodies that feel present, readable, and rooted in the street they occupy.

Collage, painting, UV light, and salvaged street posters stack into work that argues for peace without preaching.

The street is both material and message—fragments of propaganda become tender rearrangements.

Her women stare without eyes yet feel unmistakably seen—cheeky, empowered, drawn with graphic poster bones.

Solos and talks circle the globe; commercial rosters include racing teams and streaming giants without diluting the voice.

hen-macabi.heroHook: Hen Macabi is the graphic voice behind Israel’s loudest pop moments—posters and covers you already danced to.
hen-macabi.storyFullText:
Hen Macabi is an Israeli illustrator and art director whose typography-forward pieces soundtracked a generation of radio hits.

From Netta’s global breakout to club nights across the country, his covers treat type like percussion.
